titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Elizabeth Tyrrell 30, June, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell acknowledges receipt of a "splendid" parcel and reassures his mother that he has plenty of socks.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
30 June 1917 My dearest Mamy. I did not think there would be
anything for me to write about
today but yesterday evening that
splendid parcel arrived. Really
you are absolutely the best that
ever ever lived and I do not know
how to thank you enough. Everything was in perfect order.
bottle and all. You ask
me about socks. Well I have
more than enough of everything.
I have 6 pairs of heavy ones and
several of the light ones so I have
tons. There is not a sign of a
hole in any of them yet. They are lasting wonderfully
I was also delighted to get
long letters from both you
and Daddy and Minnie
yesterday. We are living here
in absolute luxury. We carry
on with just the ordinary training. There is a sort of heavy mist
today. There seems to be a change
in the weather. Minnie had not much news but
seems to be eagerly looking
forward to her holidays. You need not been thinking
about leave as there is no
possibility of it until well into
autumn. Fellows who came
out in January + earlier are only
now getting leave and even then
very few of them. Things are much
too busy for leave. Now do not be expecting another
letter tomorrow as you know that I
am quite well and there is no
other news. With very best love from Your most affec Son
Willie
